coredata.dc:title: Efficient BTMS for lithium-ion batteries: A study on PCM/Metal foam, heat pipe, and microchannel integration

coredata.dc:description:
This study introduces an innovative battery thermal management system (BTMS) that integrates liquid cooling, U-type heat pipes, and composite phase change materials (CPCM) to enhance thermal efficiency. Utilizing a transient thermo-fluid simulation model, the thermal performance of the proposed advanced BTMS for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is evaluated under various operational conditions. The model's predictions are validated against experimental data, ensuring reliability and robustness. The study examines two BTMS configurations: liquid cooling and a hybrid system combining active liquid cooling with passive CPCM and heat pipes. These configurations are examined under different thermal loads to assess their effectiveness. The study investigates the influence of liquid inlet velocity and ambient temperature on the maximum temperature (
T
m a x
) and maximum temperature difference (
Δ
T
m a x
) within the battery module, critical metrics affecting battery efficiency and lifespan. Additionally, the study evaluates the driving cycle and pumping power requirements, comparing the BTMS with alternative designs, demonstrating that the proposed system efficiently dissipates heat while requiring lower pumping power. Key findings include the superior performance of hybrid cooling, which reduces
T
m a x
by 9.32 K at 293 K and maintains
Δ
T
m a x
below 5 K. The hybrid BTMS achieves similar thermal performance with 53 % less power than liquid cooling. During active cooling failures, passive cooling with CPCM and heat pipes effectively removes heat, maintaining an optimal temperature range with passive BTMS peaking at 308.79 K and hybrid BTMS below 302 K. Under real-world driving conditions, the hybrid BTMS lowers
T
m a x
by 8.2 K and stabilizes temperature fluctuations.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
This study introduces an innovative battery thermal management system (BTMS) that integrates liquid cooling, U-type heat pipes, and composite phase change materials (CPCM) to enhance thermal efficiency. Utilizing a transient thermo-fluid simulation model, the thermal performance of the proposed advanced BTMS for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is evaluated under various operational conditions. The model's predictions are validated against experimental data, ensuring reliability and robustness. The study examines two BTMS configurations: liquid cooling and a hybrid system combining active liquid cooling with passive CPCM and heat pipes. These configurations are examined under different thermal loads to assess their effectiveness. The study investigates the influence of liquid inlet velocity and ambient temperature on the maximum temperature (
T
m a x
) and maximum temperature difference (
Δ
T
m a x
) within the battery module, critical metrics affecting battery efficiency and lifespan. Additionally, the study evaluates the driving cycle and pumping power requirements, comparing the BTMS with alternative designs, demonstrating that the proposed system efficiently dissipates heat while requiring lower pumping power. Key findings include the superior performance of hybrid cooling, which reduces
T
m a x
by 9.32 K at 293 K and maintains
Δ
T
m a x
below 5 K. The hybrid BTMS achieves similar thermal performance with 53 % less power than liquid cooling. During active cooling failures, passive cooling with CPCM and heat pipes effectively removes heat, maintaining an optimal temperature range with passive BTMS peaking at 308.79 K and hybrid BTMS below 302 K. Under real-world driving conditions, the hybrid BTMS lowers
T
m a x
by 8.2 K and stabilizes temperature fluctuations.

ce:section.ce:para:
Electric vehicles (EVs) have garnered considerable interest recently because of high energy efficiency, low noise levels, and reduced environmental impact relative to traditional fuel vehicles. The LIB is widely used as an energy storage solution in various EV platforms because of their high energy density, long lifespan, and low self-discharge rate [1]. However, LIBs are sensitive to their operating surroundings, with service life often being reduced by overly high or unfavorable temperatures. Safety issues associated with thermal runaway are also of paramount importance, particularly given their involvement in several EV accidents. Maintaining an operational temperature range of 288 K to 308 K, with a
Δ
T
m a x
of 5 K, is advised for LIBs [2]. To meet EVs' demands for elongated driving ranges and rapid recharging, the capacity and charging/discharging rates of LIBs have been continuously increased, resulting in higher heat loads. Effective temperature management of batteries is, therefore, a significant challenge [3,4]. Consequently, designing an efficient BTMS is essential to ensure the longevity and reliable performance of batteries under demanding conditions. Common cooling techniques encompass air cooling, liquid cooling, cooling with PCM, and hybrid cooling approaches [5,6]. Air cooling is widely adopted due to its uncomplicated design, affordability, and minimal maintenance requirements, fails to meet the cooling demands of batteries under harsh condition and cannot improve temperature distribution [7]. Heat pipes, despite their high heat transfer efficiency and low servicing costs, are limited by small contact areas and large system volumes. PCM cooling systems, known for energy efficiency and temperature uniformity, are constrained by limited heat dissipation capacity under high pressures [8,9]. Liquid cooling systems offer effective thermal dissipation due to higher heat capacity and thermal conductivity but incur additional pump costs and may experience temperature non-uniformity [10,11]. Consequently, hybrid cooling, which combines these methods, is emerging as a more efficient and widely implemented strategy.

Monika et al. [12] studied a cold plate with straight microchannels, finding that increasing the coolant flow rate decreased the battery module's average temperature but resulted in a notable pressure drop and higher energy consumption. Zhao et al. [13] conducted a battery simulation to identify optimal liquid flow conditions for standard charging and discharging operations. The BTMS maintained a
T
m a x
of 308 K and a temperature difference of 1 K within the battery module. Modifying cooling channel geometry and plate numbers, alongside coolant selection, are key for reducing battery module temperatures and improving uniformity, with recent advancements yielding diverse designs [14]. Chen et al. [15] developed a cooling plate featuring three U-shaped parallel-flow channels. The study demonstrated that with discharge rates of 0.5C, 1C, and 1.5C, the
T
m a x
maintained at 299 K, 305 K, and 313 K, respectively. Qi et al. [16] introduced a Swiss roll-design micro-channel TMS for power batteries. The Swiss roll BTMS achieved a
T
m a x
of 300.4 K, a temperature differential of 3.3 K, with a cooling belt height of 55 mm, improving these metrics by 0.2 K, 4 K, and 10 mm, respectively, over conventional systems. An et al. [17] enhanced the thermal regulation of prismatic LIBs through a hybrid system employing modular dual bionic cold plates. They applied the structural instances of nasturtium veins and honeycombs; this innovative design significantly improves heat dissipation and weight optimization. The primary findings demonstrate a 1.46% decrease in the
T
m a x
, a 13.92 % reduction in
Δ
T
m a x
, and a 40.28 % diminution in pressure differential. Phase change cooling provides superior temperature uniformity for LIBs compared to the traditional liquid method with paraffin materials. PCMs favored for their high energy storage, non-toxicity, adaptability, and cost-effectiveness [18,19]. Despite significant research interest, passive cooling's thermal transfer performance is limited by the low thermal conductivity of PCMs compared to active methods [20]. Zhou et al. [21] investigated how PCM's melting point temperature and thickness affect the thermal loss of LIBs. They found that lowering the melting temperature and increasing the PCM thickness improved cooling performance. However, beyond a thickness of 25 mm, the cooling efficiency showed a diminishing return. Li et al. [22] developed a novel type of CPCM to tackle challenges of leakage, insufficient thermal conductivity, and increased flammability. The CPCM variant successfully maintained the
T
m a x
and temperature difference below 323 K and 5 K under 3C and 297 K. Enhancing the thermal conductivity of PCMs in BTMS is essential for improving performance and safety. A highly effective approach involves incorporating metal foams into PCMs [23,24]. Because of their elevated thermal conductivity and extensive surface area, metal foams significantly enhance heat transfer. They provide a robust framework that helps the PCM maintain its shape while boosting thermal conductivity [25]. Additionally, conductive fillers such as graphite, carbon nanotubes, or graphene can be used. Nevertheless, metal foams are often regarded as the optimal choice due to their superior thermal properties and structural advantages, making them ideal for advanced battery management systems. Weng et al. [26] examined the thermal performance of PCM BTMS across various situations. They identified essential failure junctures for PCM across various temperature thresholds. Additionally, the study utilized other nanomaterial additives alongside EG to quantitatively raise the thermal conductivity of the PCM. Alipanah and Li [27] conducted numerical simulations to examine the BTMS of LIBs utilizing pure octadecane and an Al foam composite. The results demonstrated that integrating Al foam with octadecane extended the discharge duration by 7.3 times relative to pure octadecane, substantially enhancing temperature uniformity and minimizing the
Δ
T
m a x
. Heyhat et al. [28] investigated improving BTMS by integrating PCM with fins, nanoparticles, and metal foam. The optimal design achieved a 6 K temperature reduction using metal foam, 4 K with fins, and a 5 % decrease in melting time with 7 % Cu nanoparticles, significantly enhancing thermal management performance. Su et al. [29] developed an innovative ethylene-vinyl-acetate (EVA)-based flexible CPCM for BTMS, combining EVA, EG, and PA. With 30 wt % EVA, this CPCM reduces
T
m a x
to 322.2 K at a 4C discharge rate, improving by 287.2 K over natural convection, and maintains a
Δ
T
m a x
within 5 K. Dagdevir et al. [30] evaluated the impact of helical fins and CPCM on BTMS. By integrating 12 % expanded graphite in paraffin-based CPCM, with 6 mm wide fins and 3 fin rounds, the optimal setup reduced battery temperature by 282.3 K, keeping it under 318 K for 9316 s, extending the safe operating time by 244 %.

Conversely, heat pipes are passive TMS that offer exceptional thermal conductivity. Heat pipes have garnered significant attention as passive cooling systems due to their high thermal conductivity, cost-effectiveness, low maintenance requirements, and lightweight design [31]. Behi et al. [32] performed computational analyses to assess the effects of heat pipe and evaporative cooling on cell and module levels. Their findings revealed a reduction in the
T
m a x
by 35.8 % for the cell and 23.8 % for the module. Liang et al. [33] improved BTMS for EV using a bent flat micro heat pipe array (FMHPA). The system achieved 15.741W/m.K thermal conductivity, maintained
Δ
T
m a x
<5 K, increased temperature rise rate by 41 %, and reduced charging time by 5 min. Zheng et al. [34] examined a novel BTMS for EVs utilizing tubular heat pipes (THPs). The system reached the
T
m a x
of 327 K at a 2C discharge rate with a 288.2 K differential. THP decreased temperature variations to 3.2 K, and fin spacing was optimized to 3 mm. Wang et al. [35] investigated FHP cooling for BTMS. Their results showed
T
m a x
lower than 323 K at 3C with a 2.26 K of the
Δ
T
m a x
. Decreasing vapor cavity thickness increases thermal resistance, highlighting significant temperature uniformity and heat dissipation improvements. Ren et al. [36] enhanced the preheating TMS for LIBs using a U-shaped micro heat pipe array (MHPA). The innovation involved integrating polyimide heating films and thermal insulation properties. The results demonstrated a 386 % increase in the
T
m a x
rate from 0.0634K/hr to 0.268K/hr and a 46.7 % extension in holding time from 5.45 to 8 h. Under 32 W electric heating, the battery preheated from −253 K to 273 K in 26 min. Recent researchers in LIB cooling have focused on hybrid cooling methods. This method combines multiple techniques to enhance cooling efficiency, temperature uniformity, and overall battery performance, addressing the limitations of single-method cooling systems [37]. Chavan et al. [38] examined LIB TM using a hybrid system with heat pipes and liquid cold plates. Their study showed this approach lowered
T
m a x
by 6 K in comparison to liquid cold plates alone, enhancing temperature uniformity, reducing thermal stress, and extending battery lifespan. Chen et al. [39] investigated the efficacy of a TiO2 nanofluid-based pulsating heat pipe (PHP) for TMS of LIBs. Under 308 K ambient temperature conditions and 1C, the
T
m a x
reached 315.22 K, maintaining a temperature difference below 2 K and enhancing temperature uniformity by 60 %. Nata et al. [40] arrangement a BTMS for electric bikes utilizing CPCM and heat pipes, incorporating 20 % EG to increase thermal conductivity by 104 times. Under discharge rates of 0.5C, 1C, and 1.5C, the BTMS reduced
T
m a x
to 310 K, further decreasing to 308.25 K with forced convection at 3m/s. Leng et al. [41] examined the thermal efficiency utilizing a hybrid BTMS of HP and PCM. The proposed system achieved a surface temperature of 316 K, 24 K lower than the HP system alone, and extended the control duration by 1.7 times. The optimal application parameters are a heat transfer coefficient (h) less than 12W/m²·K and PCM thermal conductivity between 0.1 and 5W/m·K. Ping et al. [42] explored an innovative TMS integrating PCM and liquid cooling for prismatic LiFePO4 battery packs. The findings demonstrate efficient thermal regulation at 318 K, with a
T
m a x
decrease to 320.6 K and a temperature differential of 4.5 K. Karimi et al. [43] examined thermal regulation strategies for LIBs subjected to high current loads, utilizing diverse cooling methodologies. Integrating liquid-cooled BTMS and PCM, the innovative hybrid system achieves a 50.1 % reduction in peak temperature, lowering it from 337.3 K to 305.1 K while ensuring temperature uniformity within a 4.5 K range. Luo et al. [44] presented an advanced hybrid BTMS that integrates a U-shaped MHPA and a liquid cold plate incorporating CPCM to enhance temperature regulation in prismatic batteries. The findings indicated that this hybrid BTMS decreases the
T
m a x
by 4.2 K, reduces the temperature variation by 8.39 K, and lowers energy consumption by 41.7 %, substantiating its efficacy in mitigating TM challenges and energy consumption in EVs Table 1 .

This study introduces a novel BTMS designed to enhance thermal efficiency by integrating liquid cooling, U-type heat pipes, and CPCM. The thermal performance of this advanced BTMS for LIBs is assessed using a transient thermo-fluid simulation model under various operational conditions. The comprehensive simulation model includes the domains of LIBs, microchannel heat sinks, heat pipes, and CPCM to provide a detailed evaluation of the BTMS's cooling characteristics. To validate the accuracy of the developed simulation model, its predictions are meticulously compared with experimental data. This comparative analysis ensures the reliability of the simulation results and underscores the model's robustness. The study explores two distinct BTMS configurations: (1) liquid cooling and (2) a hybrid cooling system that combines active liquid cooling with passive CPCM and heat pipes. These configurations are scrutinized to understand their efficacy under different thermal loads. This research focuses on investigating how liquid inlet velocity and ambient temperature affect the
T
m a x
and
Δ
T
m a x
within the battery module. These parameters are critical performance metrics that significantly influence battery efficiency and lifespan. The study provides insights into optimizing the TMS to maintain battery health and performance by examining these factors. Moreover, the study delves into the pumping power requirements of the cooling system, comparing the BTMS with alternative designs. This analysis demonstrates that the proposed system effectively dissipates heat while requiring lower pumping power, highlighting its energy efficiency and operational cost benefits. This comprehensive evaluation showcases the BTMS's ability to maintain optimal thermal conditions. It emphasizes its potential to enhance LIBs operational reliability and longevity in real-world applications. This innovative approach sets a new benchmark BTM, providing a viable solution for the growing demands of high-performance energy storage systems. To reduce computational expense and time, a half-cell configuration is utilized in the simulation, which includes a corresponding half-cooling plate and microchannels, applying a symmetry boundary condition.

For this research, a prismatic LIB utilized in EVs was selected. This battery features a carbonate-based electrolyte, a lithium cobalt oxide (LCO) cathode, and a graphite anode, providing a capacity of 7Ah. The dimensions of the battery are 45 mm in width, 145 mm in height, and 9 mm in thickness. The geometric and thermal properties of this cell are detailed in Table 2 . This research investigation focuses on a module encompassing of 4 CPCMs, 4 heat pipes, 2 cold plates, and 3 LIBs as shown in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1 illustrates the system's configuration through a 3D schematic view (Fig. 1a) along with detailed 2D projections (Figs. 1b–1d), providing a comprehensive visualization of the module layout. A simulation uses a half-cell configuration with a corresponding half-cooling plate and microchannels to minimize computational expense and time, applying a symmetry boundary condition.

Table 3 details the cold plate's specifications, characteristics of RT-27 PCM, composite PCM/Al foam, and heat pipe used in this study. To assess the TMS, simulating a discharge rate of 5C, is analyzed. A user-defined function (UDF) code was created to compute the transient thermal generation within the LIBs.

The simulation domain is divided into five distinct zones, the fluid domain (water, PCM), heat pipe, battery cell, and cold plate. The principal equations pertinent to each sub-region are derived and formulated as follows.
•
Battery Unit

The heat production of the battery cell varies and is influenced by multiple factors, including state of charge (SOC), cell size, the rate of electrochemical reactions, temperature, and discharge rate. For this analysis, the thermal production connection with electrical variables was employed as proposed by Bernarda et al. [51]. By applying the energy equation to a correlation obtained from an extensive LIB model, the ensuing result is achieved [46]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Definition of initial and boundary conditions
ce:section.ce:para: At the start of the process, the temperature of all battery module components is assumed to be equivalent to the surrounding temperature. In this study, various environmental conditions with surrounding temperatures of 293 K, 297 K, and 303 K were evaluated to achieve an optimal TMS. Since the focus was on simulating the discharge process, the SOC of the LIB was set to 1 at the beginning and 0 at the end of the discharge process. Different inlet velocities of 0.1, 0.3, 0.7, and 1m/s were adopted for the active cooling segment. The boundary conditions for the inlet and outlet surfaces of the microchannels were set to an inlet velocity along with a pressure outlet condition. According to Table 4 , the Reynolds number was less than 2300, and the flow regime was adopted to be laminar. All other external surfaces of the LIB were considered insulated, and the internal surfaces were treated as interfaces.

To simulate the thermal behavior of the hybrid BTMS, a transient three-dimensional CFD model was developed using ANSYS Fluent. The meshing process was performed with ANSYS Meshing, generating a structured hexahedral grid to ensure computational efficiency and accuracy. The overall numerical methodology follows a structured approach, as illustrated in Fig. 2 , summarizing the key steps from model definition to validation and analysis. The governing equations, including energy, mass, and momentum conservation, were solved using a pressure-based solver, employing the SIMPLE algorithm for pressure-velocity coupling. The phase change phenomenon of CPCM was modeled using the enthalpy-porosity approach, which effectively captures the melting and solidification processes. The heat generation within the battery cells was implemented through an UDF derived from analytical models to ensure a realistic representation of the thermal source.

A structured hexahedral mesh was generated using the Sweep Method in ANSYS Meshing to ensure an optimal balance between computational accuracy and efficiency. The meshing strategy incorporated inflation layers in the fluid domain to refine the near-wall resolution, employing the Smooth Transition method with five layers and a growth rate of 1.2. The fluid domain was meshed with an average element size of 0.3 mm to resolve velocity and temperature gradients accurately, while the solid domain was assigned a slightly coarser 0.5 mm element size to reduce computational cost while preserving thermal accuracy. Edge sizing refinement was applied in critical regions, particularly around microchannels, to enhance resolution in areas with significant velocity and temperature gradients. The final structured mesh distribution, including refined regions and boundary layer inflation, is shown in Fig. 3 .

To ensure the reliability of computational results, a mesh independence study was conducted using four different grid sizes: 3259,675, 3724,573, 4502,874, and 5247,891 elements. The results of
T
m a x
and liquid fraction were analyzed for these mesh sizes. As shown in Fig. 4 , at a 5C discharge rate, ambient temperature of 297 K, and inlet velocity of 0.3m/s, the deviation in
T
m a x
and liquid fraction at 300 s between the finest mesh and the selected 3724,573-element mesh was below 4.7 % and 4.8 %, respectively. These findings confirm that the 3724,573-element mesh provides a balance between computational accuracy and efficiency.

In this study, a comprehensive validation process was conducted for both the LIB thermal model and the PCM melting to ensure the reliability and accuracy of the numerical simulations. The validation was carried out by comparing the simulation results against well-established experimental data from the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the LIB thermal model
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal performance of a 100Ah LIB under natural air-cooling was evaluated at 1C and 1.5C discharge rates with an ambient temperature of 293 K and a convective heat transfer coefficient of 5Wm⁻²K⁻¹. Validation against experimental data from Lin et al. [54] confirms the model's accuracy. Using the Bernardi heat generation model [51], the simulation accounts for Joule heating and entropy change effects. As shown in Fig. 5 , the maximum deviation was 3.5 % at 1C and 9.1 % at 1.5C, with increased deviation at higher discharge rates due to greater thermal gradients. The results suggest that natural convection alone is insufficient for high-power applications, emphasizing the need for enhanced cooling strategies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the PCM melting model
ce:section.ce:para: The numerical validation of the PCM melting process was performed using a two-dimensional computational model in ANSYS Fluent. The VOF method was employed to accurately track the phase interface and prevent phase interpenetration. The Enthalpy-Porosity model was utilized to incorporate latent heat effects and simulate the phase transition with high fidelity. The governing equations were discretized using the finite volume method, and a pressure-based solver was adopted due to the negligible density variation during phase change. The flow was assumed laminar, and the SIMPLE algorithm was implemented for pressure-velocity coupling to enhance numerical stability. A mushy zone (Amush) constant of 10⁵ was applied to represent the solid-liquid transition [55,56]. The melting process was initiated at 295 K, with the surrounding walls maintained at 330 K to establish a sufficient thermal gradient. The URF coefficients of 0.9, 0.3, and 0.5 were assigned to the liquid fraction, pressure, and momentum equations, respectively. A time step of 0.01(s), with 40 iterations per step, ensured numerical accuracy.

Based on Fig. 6 b, the PCM phase transition evolved from conduction-dominated to convection-driven melting. Initially, heat transfer occurred through a thin liquid layer, but intensified natural convection reshaped the solid-liquid interface, forming a conical shape, consistent with experimental observations and numerical predictions. As depicted in Fig. 6a, the simulated melting fraction over 18 min exhibits a maximum deviation below 4.5 % compared to experimental data from Shmueli et al. [47], confirming the model's accuracy and predictive capability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Complexity and performance considerations of the proposed hybrid BTMS
ce:section.ce:para:
The proposed hybrid BTMS integrates liquid cooling, heat pipes, and CPCM, forming a sophisticated thermal management system that optimally balances temperature regulation, uniformity, and energy efficiency. Active cooling methods, such as liquid cooling, effectively reduce peak battery temperature; however, due to the high flow velocity of the coolant, they fail to achieve uniform temperature distribution within the battery module. Conversely, passive cooling systems, including heat pipes and CPCM, offer superior temperature uniformity (
Δ
T
m a x
< 5 K) but lack precise control over peak battery temperature. Moreover, active cooling requires continuous energy input, leading to increased operational costs due to pump power consumption. To address these limitations, the hybrid BTMS strategically combines these technologies. Liquid cooling efficiently dissipates heat via microchannels and cold plates, heat pipes passively redistribute heat, and CPCM absorbs excess thermal energy, preventing sudden temperature increases. The integration of metal foam within CPCM enhances thermal conductivity, further optimizing heat transfer efficiency. This system successfully maintains
T
m a x
below 305 K and ensures
Δ
T
m a x
of less than 2 K across the battery module. While this multi-layered approach increases system complexity, it reduces pumping power consumption by 53 % compared to conventional liquid cooling systems. This integrated design demonstrates that leveraging the advantages of both active and passive cooling technologies can achieve an optimized BTMS, ensuring improved thermal stability, operational reliability, and extended battery lifespan.

The
T
m a x
and
Δ
T
m a x
of the battery pack are the most important parameters that directly impact the battery pack's performance, longevity, and safety. Fig. 7 shows the variation of the
T
m a x
with respect to inlet velocity at a 5C discharge rate and three ambient temperatures: 293 K, 297 K, and 303 K for both active liquid cooling and hybrid cooling BTMS. As demonstrated, increasing inlet velocity causes a lower
T
m a x
in both designed BTMS due to a higher convection heat transfer coefficient. Hybrid cooling in a 293 K ambient temperature decreases the
T
m a x
by 9.32 K at 0.1m/s inlet velocity. However, raising the inlet velocity to 1m/s leads to a lower difference in
T
m a x
between hybrid and liquid cooling, with the difference equal to 7.66 K, owing to the constant rate of battery discharge. As shown in Fig. 7, similar results are achieved in the 297 K and 303 K ambient temperature simulations, with hybrid cooling reducing the
T
m a x
by 8.19 K and 6.21 K, respectively. Results show that by increasing the ambient temperature, the hybrid cooling heat dissipation performance decreases, mainly because of the CPCM melting process in higher ambient temperatures, especially at 303 K, where the CPCM melts completely before the discharge process starts. Fig. 8 shows the temperature distribution of inner battery cell for hybrid BTMS at 297 K ambient temperature at a range of inlet velocities during three stages of the discharge process. As shown, increasing inlet velocity leads to a reduction in temperature inside the battery cell. Additionally, temperature distribution and
Δ
T
m a x
inside the batteries are within the allowable range, below 5 K.

The
Δ
T
m a x
becomes an important factor in a battery pack, affecting the pack's uniform output voltage and extending its life cycle. Fig. 9 demonstrated the
Δ
T
m a x
of battery cells in the designed module at an environmental temperature of 297 K and various simulated inlet velocities. As depicted, for all simulated scenarios, the
Δ
T
m a x
remains below 5 K. Furthermore, increasing inlet velocity magnitude from 0.1m/s to 1m/s results in a rise from 1.12 K to 1.69 K for liquid cooling and from 1.46 K to 3.28 K for hybrid BTMS. Due to the low thermal conductivity of the batteries, increasing the inlet velocity results in a reduction in temperature near the cold plate, while the core temperature of the batteries remains unchanged. Implementing heat pipes attached to the core of the batteries addresses concerns about the batteries' low thermal conductivity, reducing the core temperature without requiring additional pumping power. Additionally, in zones where attaching heat pipes is impractical, CPCM is deployed to dissipate the heat generated by the batteries.

Using CPCM for heat dissipation in BTMS has become increasingly attractive in recent years. However, the optimal volume of CPCM must be deployed in BTMS to prevent excessive weight increase. Fig. 10 shows the CPCM liquid fraction contours at an ambient temperature of 297 K and inlet velocities of 0.1, 0.3, and 1m/s at 200, 400, and 600 s after the discharge process started. As illustrated, for all inlet velocities, the CPCM completely melts before the battery is fully discharged.

Another critical parameter in the BTMS heat dissipation process is the required pumping power to flow coolant. Hybrid cooling strategy main focus is on the
T
m a x
and
Δ
T
m a x
reduction, beside reducing pumping power is another effect of hybrid BTMS. Fig. 11 illustrates the
T
m a x
and pumping power for liquid cooling and hybrid BTMS at various inlet velocities under a 5C discharge rate. It can be observed that pumping power increases with higher inlet velocities. Additionally, the
T
m a x
in the liquid BTMS at an inlet velocity of 1m/s is approximately equal to that in the hybrid BTMS at an inlet velocity of 0.7m/s. This indicates that the two proposed BTMS configurations achieve comparable thermal performance at different inlet velocities, while the pumping power required by the hybrid BTMS is 53 % lower than that of the liquid BTMS. Consequently, a hybrid BTMS design can achieve similar thermal efficiency with significantly lower pumping power compared to active cooling methods. Akbarzadeh et al. [57] proposed a hybrid BTMS incorporating CPCM alongside an active liquid cooling system, resulting in a 45 % reduction in pumping power while current study achieved more efficient thermal-hydraulic performance.

In the case of failure of the active cooling system caused by lack of pumping power or blockage of the mini-channels, the burden of heat removal depends solely on the passive cooling system with CPCM and heat pipes. The thermal performance of the CPCM and heat pipes in the absence of active cooling was studied at a discharge rate of 5C, an ambient temperature of 293 K, and zero water velocity. The optimum LIB operation temperature range is 288.15 K to 308.15 K, where above 343.15 K, battery cells are unstable, with the generation of smoke beside very high and risks of explosion. Fig. 12 shows the
T
m a x
of the batteries for the passive-based (where inlet velocity set to 0m/s) and hybrid BTMS (where coolant flow through microchannels) configurations alongside the liquid fraction of the CPCM in both systems. The passive-based BTMS peaks at 308.79 K, at the end of discharge process, and the optimal temperature range never exceed allowable range. In comparison with the hybrid cooling system, the temperature of the battery is maintained within the temperature range below 302 K.

As illustrated in Fig. 12, the CPCM undergoes complete melting within 200 s, prior to the batteries reaching full discharge. This indicates that incorporating a larger volume of CPCM within the BTMS could enhance its efficiency, thereby extending the battery's safe operating duration. However, it is important to consider that the weight of the cooling system remains a critical objective. Fig. 13 depicts the temperature contour of battery cells under the safety scenario and hybrid BTMS at 200 s, 400 s, and 600 s. The results indicate that despite the lack of forced convection, the passive system effectively maintains battery temperature within the operational limits, preventing thermal runaway. A gradual temperature increase is observed over time, yet the passive BTMS mitigates excessive heating. In contrast, the hybrid BTMS ensures a more uniform temperature distribution with lower peak values due to the combined effect of active and passive cooling. These findings emphasize the reliability of the passive system in maintaining thermal safety under active cooling failure conditions.

Recent research has primarily concentrated on evaluating the effectiveness of BTMS under steady heat generation or uniform discharge rates. Nevertheless, actual heat production in batteries is closely associated with driving patterns, leading to considerable variations. This section addresses the assessment of variable heat generation as reflective of real-world battery operating scenarios, and the performance of BTMS is analyzed under these dynamic conditions. Utilizing data on heat generation in a LIB during a driving cycle (WLTP class 3), a volumetric heat generation rate was determined. Fig. 14 illustrates the heat generation rate at different driving times. The heat generation rate does not surpass 190000W/m3 and exhibits a fluctuating pattern throughout the 5800-second discharge process. Fig. 15 (a) and (b) respectively display the
T
m a x
,
Δ
T
m a x
, and PCM liquid phase ratio.

The
T
m a x
in the hybrid BTMS is 8.2 K lower than that of the active BTMS. Furthermore, the presence of PCM resulted in more consistent temperature fluctuations in the hybrid cooling system, with a range of 299.5 K to 302.5 K, whereas in the active BTMS, the fluctuation range extended from 304.5 K to 311.7 K. The liquid phase ratio exceeded 70 % after 1400 s and fully liquefied two times.

The
Δ
T
m a x
suggests that this parameter remains relatively stable across both cooling strategies. However, the hybrid BTMS exhibits lower
Δ
T
m a x
. There is a shortage of research on the impact of temperature fluctuations on battery performance. This study presents a new battery module design with compact cell placement and an efficient cooling system to extend EV driving range and reduce charging time. It also highlights the need for research on high thermal conductive materials for safety and optimized thermal systems to reduce cooling system weight and pumping power.

This study presents an advanced hybrid battery thermal management system integrating liquid cooling, U-type heat pipes, and CPCM to enhance the thermal performance of LIBs. The system's effectiveness was evaluated through a transient thermo-fluid simulation model, which was validated against experimental data to ensure accuracy and robustness. Two distinct BTMS configurations were analyzed: (1) a conventional liquid cooling system and (2) a hybrid cooling system combining active liquid cooling with passive CPCM and heat pipes. The study examined their thermal performance under various thermal loads, inlet velocities, and ambient temperatures, focusing on key parameters such as maximum battery temperature and maximum temperature difference. Additionally, the system's pumping power requirements and performance under real-world driving conditions (WLTP Class 3) were investigated, demonstrating that the hybrid BTMS significantly improves heat dissipation, enhances temperature uniformity, and reduces energy consumption compared to conventional liquid cooling. The key finding of this study is summarized as follows:
•
Superior Temperature Reduction: The hybrid BTMS demonstrated enhanced heat dissipation capabilities compared to liquid cooling. At an ambient temperature of 293 K, the hybrid system reduced
T
m a x
by 9.32 K at an inlet velocity of 0.1m/s, while at 297 K and 303 K, the reductions were 8.19 K and 6.21 K, respectively. However, at higher ambient temperatures, the heat dissipation efficiency of CPCM decreased due to premature melting.
•
Improved Temperature Uniformity: The hybrid cooling system maintained the
Δ
T
m a x
within the critical 5K threshold across all scenarios. At an inlet velocity of 0.1m/s,
Δ
T
m a x
was 1.46 K for the hybrid BTMS and 1.12 K for liquid cooling, while at 1m/s,
Δ
T
m a x
increased to 3.28 K and 1.69 K, respectively. The integration of heat pipes significantly improved core temperature uniformity, addressing the challenge of low thermal conductivity in battery materials.
•
Energy Efficiency and Pumping Power Reduction: The hybrid BTMS achieved thermal performance equivalent to liquid cooling while consuming 53 % less pumping power. At an inlet velocity of 0.7m/s, the hybrid BTMS maintained similar cooling effectiveness as liquid cooling at 1m/s, highlighting its superior thermal efficiency and reduced energy consumption.
•
Passive Cooling and Thermal Safety: In scenarios where active cooling failed, the passive BTMS maintained a peak temperature of 308.79 K, preventing thermal runaway. The hybrid BTMS further enhanced safety by keeping the
T
m a x
below 302 K, confirming the effectiveness of CPCM and heat pipes in improving reliability under critical conditions.
•
Performance Under Real-Driving Conditions: Under a WLTP Class 3 driving cycle, hybrid BTMS lowered
T
m a x
by 8.2 K compared to liquid cooling. Additionally, the hybrid system stabilized temperature fluctuations within a range of 299.5 K to 302.5 K, while in liquid cooling, fluctuations ranged from 304.5 K to 311.7 K. This highlights the superiority of the hybrid BTMS in managing dynamic thermal loads, reducing excessive temperature variations during high-power driving cycles.

This study establishes the efficacy of a hybrid BTMS integrating liquid cooling, heat pipes, and CPCM; however, further advancements are required for enhanced performance and real-world applicability. Future research should focus on developing high-thermal-conductivity CPCM to improve heat dissipation while mitigating system weight constraints. Optimizing heat pipe configurations and microchannel geometries through CFD and experimental validation could enhance thermal uniformity and minimize temperature gradients. Additionally, implementing real-time adaptive control algorithms utilizing machine learning could optimize BTMS performance under dynamic load conditions. Experimental validation under real-world driving cycles is crucial to ensure system reliability and robustness. Furthermore, multi-objective optimization frameworks, incorporating thermo-hydraulic efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and manufacturability, should be explored to facilitate large-scale adoption in next-generation electric vehicles. These advancements will be pivotal in bridging the gap between computational modeling and practical implementation, ultimately enhancing battery safety, longevity, and overall system efficiency.
